questions.question: Explain the ACID properties of a database transaction. For each property, provide a practical example demonstrating its importance in maintaining data integrity during a funds transfer operation between two bank accounts.
keyPoints: Atomicity: All operations in a transaction complete successfully or none do; funds transfer must debit one account and credit another as a single unit
Consistency: Database moves from one valid state to another; after transfer, total balance remains unchanged
Isolation: Concurrent transactions appear to execute serially; intermediate states not visible to other transactions
Durability: Once committed, changes persist even system failure; recorded in persistent storage

questions.question: Given a relation Employee(EmpID, Name, Department, Salary, Location) with repeated department and location data causing update anomalies, demonstrate how normalization to Third Normal Form (3NF) eliminates these anomalies. Show the decomposed relations and justify your design.
keyPoints: Identify transitive dependency: Location depends on Department, which depends on EmpID
First Normal Form: Ensure atomic values, no repeating groups
Second Normal Form: Remove partial dependencies on composite primary key
Third Normal Form: Remove transitive dependencies by decomposing into Employee(EmpID, Name, DeptID), Department(DeptID, Department, Location)

questions.question: Consider a university database with relations: Student(StudentID, Name, Email, Phone, DepartmentID), Department(DepartmentID, DepartmentName, HOD), Enrollment(StudentID, CourseID, Grade), Course(CourseID, CourseName, Credits, DepartmentID). Write SQL queries to: (a) Find departments with more than 100 students enrolled, (b) Update HOD name using a subquery, (c) Create a view showing student names with their course grades, (d) Add a new column 'Attendance' to Course table with default value 0, (e) List courses with average grade below passing threshold using HAVING clause.
keyPoints: Query (a): Use JOIN with GROUP BY and HAVING COUNT > 100 on Department and Enrollment tables
Query (b): Use UPDATE with SET and WHERE using subquery on Department table
Query (c): Use CREATE VIEW joining Student, Enrollment, and Course with appropriate column selection
Query (d): Use ALTER TABLE with ADD COLUMN syntax including DEFAULT constraint; Query (e): Use AVG(Grade) with GROUP BY and HAVING conditions

questions.question: In a banking system handling concurrent transactions, explain how the Timestamp-Based Concurrency Control protocol prevents dirty read and non-repeatable read anomalies. Given a scenario where two transactions T1 (reads balance, adds interest) and T2 (reads balance, deducts service charge) execute concurrently on the same account with timestamps TS(T1)=10 and TS(T2)=20, demonstrate step-by-step execution showing how the protocol resolves conflicts and maintains serializability.
keyPoints: Timestamp ordering assigns unique timestamps to transactions; Read_TS(X) and Write_TS(X) track latest read/write operations
If transaction tries to read data written by younger transaction (later timestamp), it is aborted and restarted
If transaction tries to write data read by younger transaction, causing non-repeatable read, it is aborted
In the scenario: T1 reads at timestamp 10; when T2 attempts write after T1's read, T2's Write_TS(20) > Read_TS(10), so T2 must wait or restart, ensuring T1 completes before T2's write
